top_issues.title: Clutch and dual-mass flywheel failure on manual variants
top_issues.description: Manual CR-V models, particularly the 1.6 i-DTEC and 2.2 i-DTEC, have experienced premature clutch failure and dual-mass flywheel (DMF) problems. Some owners report failure as early as 10,000 miles, whilst others see issues at 18,000 or 37,000 miles. Honda has attributed some failures to driver error or moisture on the clutch plate, which it has labelled a 'characteristic' of the car rather than a defect. Replacement costs are substantial and the problem can recur shortly after repair.
typical_symptoms: Judder when pulling away from cold start
Repeated clutch failure within a few thousand miles of replacement

top_issues.title: DPF blockage and regeneration failure on 1.6 i-DTEC diesel
top_issues.description: The 1.6 i-DTEC diesel engine is prone to diesel particulate filter (DPF) sooting and regeneration failure, particularly in cars used for frequent short journeys or low-mileage driving. Owners report DPF warning lights appearing within the first few thousand miles, and forced regeneration by dealers does not always resolve the issue permanently. Some cars have required complete DPF replacement under warranty, whilst others have seen the fault return shortly after repair.
typical_symptoms: Reduced engine performance or limp mode
DPF light reappearing days or weeks after dealer service

top_issues.title: Electrical and engine management faults on 2016 models
top_issues.description: Several 2016 CR-V models have developed intermittent electrical and engine management issues, including glow plug failures, Body Control Module (BCM) power drain, and PGM-FI warning lights. Some cars have required multiple visits to dealerships for diagnosis and repair, with issues sometimes recurring after work has been completed. These faults appear to be related to how the car was driven by the previous owner or manufacturing inconsistencies.
typical_symptoms: Battery draining when car is switched off

top_issues.title: Fuel system contamination and high-pressure pump failure
top_issues.description: Some CR-V models have developed fuel system problems including metal swarf contamination and high-pressure fuel pump failure. These issues typically require replacement of multiple fuel system components including the fuel pump, injectors, fuel rail, and high-pressure pipes. Costs can be substantial. Contamination may result from misfuelling or use of low-quality diesel, but some failures appear to occur on properly maintained vehicles.

top_issues.title: Excessive oil consumption on 1.6 i-DTEC diesel engines
top_issues.description: Some 1.6 i-DTEC diesel CR-V models have exhibited abnormally high oil consumption, burning through a litre of oil every 800–900 miles. Honda has stated this falls within 'normal tolerances' for the engine, particularly during active DPF regeneration cycles. The issue appears linked to short-journey driving patterns that trigger frequent regeneration, and may worsen if the engine is switched off mid-regeneration.
typical_symptoms: Low oil level on dipstick check
No visible oil leaks underneath the car

overall_reliability_note: The Honda CR-V is generally a reliable and practical family SUV, but the 1.6 i-DTEC diesel engine has shown susceptibility to clutch failure on manuals, DPF regeneration problems on short-run drivers, and occasional electrical faults on 2016 models; the older 2.2 i-DTEC and 2.0 petrol engines are best avoided.
